title: Ratios and units of measurement
description: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units (mostly US customary). Transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.

assessment_items.question_content:
A gallon of Moo Milk costs $\$5.12$.  

**What is the price, in dollars, of an $8$ ounce glass of Moo Milk?**  
*There are $128$ ounces in $1$ gallon.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The ratio of $\blueE{\text{gallons}}$  to $\purpleE{\text{cost}}$  is $\blueE{1}:\$\purpleE{5.12}$.

We want to know the ratio of $\maroonD{\text{ounces}} $ to $\purpleE{\text{cost}}$.

We need to convert gallons to ounces.

There are $\maroonD{128}$ ounces in $\blueE1$ gallon.

So, if  $\blueE{1}$ gallon of Moo Milk costs $\$\purpleE{5.12}$, then $\maroonD{128}$ ounces also cost $\$\purpleE{5.12}$.

The ratio $\maroonD{\text{ounces}}$ to $\purpleE{\text{cost}}$   is $\maroonD{128}:\$\purpleE{5.12}$.

Now, we can find an equivalent ratio to find the cost for $\maroonD{8}$ ounces.

Ounces | Cost
:-: | :-:
$\maroonD{128}$ | $\purpleE{5.12}$
$\downarrow \div 16$ | $\downarrow \div 16$
$\maroonD{8}$| $\purpleE{0.32}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]
An $8$ ounce glass of Moo Milk costs  $\$\purpleE{0.32}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
There are $86{,}400$ frames of animation in $1$ hour of anime. 

**How many frames are there per _second_**?  
*There are $3{,}600$ seconds in $1$ hour.*

 [[☃ numeric-input 1]]  frames
hints:
The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{frames}}$  to $\blueE{\text{hours}}$  is $\purpleE{86{,}400}:\blueE{1}$.

We want to know the ratio of $\purpleE{\text{frames}} $ to $\maroonE{\text{seconds}}$.

We need to convert hours to seconds.

There are $\maroonE{3{,}600}$ seconds in $\blueE1$ hour.

So, if there are $\purpleE{86{,}400}$ frames of animation in $\blueE{1}$ hour of anime, then there are also  $\purpleE{86{,}400}$ frames of animation in $\maroonE{3{,}600}$ seconds.

The ratio $\purpleE{\text{frames}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{seconds}}$   is $\purpleE{86{,}400}:\maroonE{3{,}600}$.

Now, we can find an equivalent ratio to find the frames in $\maroonE1$ second.

Frames | Seconds
:-: | :-:
$\purpleE{86{,}400}$ | $\maroonE{3{,}600}$
$\downarrow \div 3{,}600$ | $\downarrow \div 3{,}600$
$\purpleE{24}$ | $\maroonE{1}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]
There are $24$ frames of animation in $1$ second.

assessment_items.question_content:
Saqui buys streamers for $\$0.09$ per meter.

**What is the price, in dollars, of $300$ *centimeters* of streamers?**  
*There are $100$ centimeters in a meter.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The ratio of $\blueE{\text{meters}}$ of streamers to $\purpleE{\text{cost}}$ of streamers is $\blueE{1}:\purpleE{0.09}$.

We want to know the ratio of $\maroonD{\text{centimeters}}$ of streamers to $\purpleE{\text{cost}}$ of streamers.

We need to convert meters to centimeters.

There are $\maroonD{100}$ centimeters in $\blueE1$ meter.

So, if $\blueE1$ meter of streamers costs $\$\purpleE{0.09}$, then $\maroonD{100}$ centimeters of streamers also costs $\$\purpleE{0.09}$.

The ratio $\maroonD{\text{centimeters}}$ of streamers to $\purpleE{\text{cost}}$ of streamers is $\maroonD{100}:\purpleE{0.09}$.

Now, we can find an equivalent ratio to find the cost for $\maroonD{300}$ centimeters of streamers.

Length (centimeters) | Cost (dollars)
:-: | :-:
$\maroonE{100}$ | $\purpleE{0.09}$
$\downarrow \times 3$ | $\downarrow \times 3$
$\maroonE{300}$ | $\purpleE{0.27}$
The streamers cost $\$\purpleE{0.27}$ per $300$ centimeters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Amir can run $15$ kilometers in one hour.

**How many kilometers can Amir run per *minute*?**  
*There are $60$ minutes in $1$ hour.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{kilometers}}$  to $\maroonE{\text{hours}}$  is $\purpleE{15}:\maroonE{1}$.

We want to know the ratio of $\purpleE{\text{kilometers}}$  to $\maroonE{\text{minutes}}$.

We need to convert hours to minutes.

There are $\maroonE{60}$ minutes in $\purpleE1$ hour.

So, if Amir can run $\purpleE{15}$ kilometers in  $\purpleE{1}$ hour, then he can also run $\purpleE{15}$ kilometers in $\maroonE{60}$ minutes.

The ratio $\purpleE{\text{kilometers}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{minutes}}$  is $\purpleE{15}:\maroonE{60}$.

Now, we can find an equivalent ratio to find the number of kilometers for $\maroonE1$ minute.

Time (Minutes) | Distance (kilometers)
:-: | :-:
$\maroonE{60}$ | $\purpleE{15}$
$\downarrow \div 60$ | $\downarrow \div 60$
$\maroonE{1}$ | $\purpleE{0.25}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Amir can run  $\purpleE{0.25}$ kilometers per minute.

assessment_items.question_content:
There are about $946$ milliliters in a quart.  There are $2$ pints in a quart.  

**About how many milliliters are in a pint?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] milliliters
hints:
The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{milliliters}}$ to $\goldE{\text{quarts}}$ is $\purpleE{946}:\goldE{1}$.

We want to know the ratio of $\purpleE{\text{milliliters}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{pints}}$.

There are $\maroonE{2}$ pints in $\goldE{1}$ quart.

Since $\goldE{1}$ quart is $\purpleE{946}$ milliliters, $\maroonE{2}$ pints is also $\purpleE{946}$ milliliters.

The ratio of  $\purpleE{\text{milliliters}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{pints}}$  is $\purpleE{946}:\maroonE{2}$.

Now, we can find an equivalent ratio to find how many $\purpleE{\text{milliliters}}$ are in $\maroonE{\text{1 pint}}$.

Pints | Milliliters
:-: | :-:
$\maroonE{2}$ | $\purpleE{946}$
$\downarrow \div 2$ | $\downarrow \div 2$
$\maroonE{1}$ | $\purpleE{473}$
There are about $\purpleE{473}$ milliliters in $\maroonE{{1}}$ pint.

assessment_items.question_content:
There are about $30.5$ centimeters in a foot.  There are $3$ feet in a yard.  

**About how many centimeters are in a yard?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{centimeters}}$ to $\goldE{\text{feet}}$ is $\purpleE{30.5}:\goldE{1}$.

We want to know the ratio of $\purpleE{\text{centimeters}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{yards}}$.

There are $\goldE{3}$ feet in $\maroonE{1}$ yard.

If we have $3$ times as many feet, we must have $3$ times as many centimeters.

Feet | Centimeters
:-: | :-:
$\goldE{1}$ | $\purpleE{30.5}$
$\downarrow \times 3$ | $\downarrow \times 3$
$\goldE{3}$ | $\purpleE{91.5}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Since there are about $\purpleE{91.5}$ centimeters in $\goldE{3}$ feet, there are also about $\purpleE{91.5}$ centimeters in $\maroonE{1}$ yard.
There are about $\purpleE{91.5}$ centimeters in $\maroonE{{1}}$ yard.

assessment_items.question_content:
There are about $454$ grams in a pound.  There are $16$ ounces in a pound.  

**About how many grams are in an ounce?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{grams}}$ to $\goldE{\text{pounds}}$ is $\purpleE{454}:\goldE{1}$.

We want to know the ratio of $\purpleE{\text{grams }}$ to $\maroonE{\text{ounces}}$.

There are $\maroonE{16}$ ounces in $\goldE1$ pound.

Since $\goldE1$ pound is $\purpleE{454}$ grams, $\maroonE{16}$ ounces is also $\purpleE{454}$ grams.

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{grams}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{ounces}}$ is $\purpleE{454}:\maroonE{16}$.

Now, we can find an equivalent ratio to find how many $\purpleE{\text{grams}}$ are in $\maroonE{\text{1 ounce}}$.

Ounces | Grams
:-: | :-:
$\maroonE{16}$ | $\purpleE{454}$
$\downarrow \div 16$ | $\downarrow \div 16$
$\maroonE{1}$ | $\purpleE{28.375}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]
There are about $\purpleE{28.375}$ grams in $\maroonE{{1}}$ ounce.

assessment_items.question_content:
There are $128$ ounces in a gallon.  There are $4$ quarts in a gallon.  

**How many ounces are in a quart?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] ounces
hints:
The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{ounces }}$ to $\goldE{\text{gallons}}$ is $\purpleE{128}:\goldE{1}$.

We want to know the ratio of $\purpleE{\text{ounces }}$ to $\maroonE{\text{quarts}}$.

There are $\maroonE{4}$ quarts in $\goldE1$ gallon.

Since $\goldE1$ gallon is $\purpleE{128}$ ounces, $\maroonE{4}$ quarts is also $\purpleE{128}$ ounces.

The ratio of $\purpleE{\text{ounces}}$ to $\maroonE{\text{quarts}}$ to  is $\purpleE{128}:\maroonE{4}$.

Now, we can find an equivalent ratio to find how many $\purpleE{\text{ounces}}$ are in $\maroonE{\text{1 quart}}$.

Quarts | Ounces
:-: | :-:
$\maroonE{4}$ | $\purpleE{128}$
$\downarrow \div 4$ | $\downarrow \div 4$
$\maroonE{1}$ | $\purpleE{32}$
There are $\purpleE{32}$ ounces in $\maroonE{{1}}$ quart.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number lines show the ratio of feet to miles.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many feet are in $3$ miles?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 3]] feet
hints:
The number lines show that $\goldE{\text{feet}}$ to $\blueE{\text{miles}}$ can be shown with the ratio $\goldE{10{,}560}:\blueE{2}$.

We are looking for a ratio that is equivalent to $\goldE{10{,}560}:\blueE{2}$.

We can first divide by $2$ to see how many $\goldE{\text{feet}}$ are in $\blueE1$ mile.

[[☃ image 1]]

Then, we can multiply by $3$ to see how many $\goldE{\text{feet}}$ are in $\blueE3$ miles.

[[☃ image 2]]
There are $\goldE{15{,}840}$ feet in $\blueE3$ miles.

options.alt: A double number line with 4 equally spaced tick marks. The line labeled Feet, reads from left to right: 0, an unlabeled tick mark, 10,560, an unlabeled tick mark. The line labeled Miles, reads from left to right: 0, an unlabeled tick mark, 2, an unlabeled tick mark.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number lines show the ratio of cups to gallons. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many cups are in $3$ gallons?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 3]] cups
hints:
The number lines show that $\goldE{\text{cups}}$ to $\blueE{\text{gallons}}$ can be shown with the ratio $\goldE{64}:\blueE{4}$.

We are looking for a ratio that is equivalent to $\goldE{64}:\blueE{4}$.

We can first divide by $4$ to see how many $\goldE{\text{cups}}$ are in $\blueE1$ gallon.

[[☃ image 1]]

Then, we can multiply by $3$ to see how many $\goldE{\text{cups}}$ are in $\blueE3$ gallons.

[[☃ image 2]]
There are $\goldE{48}$ cups in $\blueE3$ gallons.

options.alt: A double number line with 5 equally spaced tick marks. The line labeled Cups, reads from left to right: 0, three unlabeled tick marks, 64. The line labeled Gallons, reads from left to right: 0, three unlabeled tick marks, 4.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number lines show the ratio of yards to miles. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many yards are in $4$ miles?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 3]] yards
hints:
The number lines show that $\goldE{\text{yards}}$ to $\blueE{\text{miles}}$ can be shown with the ratio $\goldE{8{,}800}:\blueE{5}$.

We are looking for a ratio that is equivalent to $\goldE{8{,}800}:\blueE{5}$.

We can first divide by $5$ to see how many $\goldE{\text{yards}}$ are in $\blueE1$ mile.

[[☃ image 1]]

Then, we can multiply by $4$ to see how many $\goldE{\text{yards}}$ are in $\blueE{4}$ miles.

[[☃ image 2]]
There are $\goldE{7{,}040}$ yards in $\blueE{4}$ miles.

options.alt: A double number line with 6 equally spaced tick marks. The line labeled Yards, reads from left to right: 0, four unlabeled tick marks, 8,800. The line labeled Miles, reads from left to right: 0, four unlabeled tick marks, 5.

assessment_items.question_content:
The double number lines show the ratio of minutes to days.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many minutes are in $2$ days?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 3]] minutes
hints:
The number lines show that $\goldE{\text{minutes}}$ to $\blueE{\text{days}}$ can be shown with the ratio $\goldE{4{,}320}:\blueE{3}$.

We are looking for a ratio that is equivalent to $\goldE{4{,}320}:\blueE{3}$.

We can first divide by $3$ to see how many $\goldE{\text{minutes}}$ are in $\blueE1$ day.

[[☃ image 1]]

Then, we can multiply by $2$ to see how many $\goldE{\text{minutes}}$ are in $\blueE2$ days.

[[☃ image 2]]
There are $\goldE{2{,}880}$ minutes in $\blueE2$ days.

options.alt: A double number line with 5 equally spaced tick marks. The line labeled Minutes, reads from left to right: 0, two unlabeled tick marks, 4,320, an unlabeled tick mark. The line labeled Days, reads from left to right: 0, two unlabeled tick marks, 3, an unlabeled tick mark.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the ratio table to convert the units of measure from centimeters to inches or inches to centimeters.**

Centimeters | Inches
:-: | :-:
$2.54$ | $1$
$5.08$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $10$
hints:
The ratio table shows that the ratio of $\redE{\text{centimeters}}$ to $\purpleD{\text{inches}}$ is $\redE{2.54}:\purpleD{1}$.

We are looking for ratios that are equivalent to $\redE{2.54}:\purpleD{1}$.

If there are $2$ times as many centimeters, there are also $2$ times as many inches.

Centimeters | Inches
:-: | :-:
$\redE{2.54}$ | $\purpleD{1}$
$\downarrow \times 2$ | $\downarrow \times 2$
$\redE{5.08}$ | $\purpleD{2}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

There are $10$ times as many inches, so there are also $10$ times as many centimeters.

Centimeters | Inches
:-: | :-:
$\redE{2.54}$ | $\purpleD{1}$
$\downarrow \times 10$ | $\downarrow \times 10$
$\redE{25.4}$ | $\purpleD{10}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The complete table:

Centimeters | Inches
:-: | :-:  
$2.54$ | $1$
$5.08$| $2$
$25.4$| $10$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the ratio table to convert the units of measure from ounces to grams or grams to ounces.**

Ounces| Grams
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $28$
$3$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $140$
hints:
The ratio table shows that the ratio of $\redE{\text{ounces}}$ to $\purpleD{\text{grams}}$ is $\redE{1}:\purpleD{28}$.

We are looking for ratios that are equivalent to $\redE{1}:\purpleD{28}$.

If there are $3$ times as many ounces, there are also $3$ times as many grams.

Ounces | Grams
:-: | :-:
$\redE{1}$ | $\purpleD{28}$
$\downarrow \times 3$ | $\downarrow \times 3$
$\redE{3}$ | $\purpleD{84}$

There are $5$ times as many grams, so there are also $5$ times as many ounces.

Ounces | Grams
:-: | :-:
$\redE{1}$ | $\purpleD{28}$
$\downarrow \times 5$ | $\downarrow \times 5$
$\redE{5}$ | $\purpleD{140}$

The complete table:

Ounces| Grams
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $28$
$3$| $84$
$5$ | $140$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Complete the ratio table to convert the units of time from hours to weeks or weeks to hours.**

Hours| Weeks
:-: | :-:
$168$ | $1$
$1{,}008$ | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
[[☃ numeric-input 2]] | $5$
hints:
The ratio table shows that the ratio of $\redE{\text{hours}}$ to $\purpleD{\text{weeks}}$ is $\redE{168}:\purpleD{1}$.

We are looking for ratios that are equivalent to $\redE{168}:\purpleD{1}$.

If there are $6$ times as many hours, there are also $6$ times as many weeks.

Hours | Weeks
:-: | :-:
$\redE{168}$ | $\purpleD{1}$
$\downarrow \times 6$ | $\downarrow \times 6$
$\redE{1{,}008}$ | $\purpleD{6}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

There are $5$ times as many weeks, so there are also $5$ times as many hours.

Hours | Weeks
:-: | :-:
$\redE{168}$ | $\purpleD{1}$
$\downarrow \times 5$ | $\downarrow \times 5$
$\redE{840}$ | $\purpleD{5}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]
